I want to dig into this question asking session in a different way You're really there's this big picture questions and there's small picture questions, right? There's non-invasive questions and Invasive questions, right? And if you've had any truth if you just knew to this go into the library that discussed this many times in the past right? Meaning big picture questions are things that you know, just you know items that really abroad, you know over all view of the company Little picture is that we get really now into something questions about a specific feature or benefit That's a different or specific pain point, right? And then your Non-invasive questions are questions that anybody would feel comfortable answering So tell me how long you're living in the area for versus. What did you pay for your house? What was the price you paid? How big is your mortgage? How much insurance do you have right now? That you know, that's an invasive question, you know How long you've been speaking about getting insurance? That's a non-invasive question. How much do you have? No, how long you how long you've been investing in the stock market non-invasive? Okay, how much do you invest in each individual? What do you have in the market right now? You get it. How many programs do a three or four non-invasive? So if these non-invasive questions and you have invasive questions when you are qualifying This is basic simple old hat stuff. I want to just reinforce it We'll get something else in a second or take it to an angle But the idea is is that when you go from non-invasive To invasive what it does is you're using these non-invasive questions to strengthen the rapport You're building rapport through these non-invasive questions because as they add to it Uh-huh. Yeah, oh, uh-huh. Got it. Yep And as you answer them and use your active listening skills, you're building this ultra tight rapport and What that does is it essentially gives you the currency in their rapport bank that Earns you the right to start asking more invasive questions Those you've earned the right now because they trust you they feel more connected to you If you just come in with all guns blazing and you start asking these really invasive questions People get offended and they hang up doesn't work So you have to ask these non-invasive questions first Makes sense. It's pretty obvious right. We've been through this many times, right? Let me take now a different side of the equation here There's something called specific questions Meaning these are questions that are specific to your industry And every industry is different. For example, if you're Dealing with people I just trained a company that was dealing with them Hosting your websites had to do with things like upload speeds, right customer service Downtime all these things that are specific to the industry, right? There's gonna be certain sets of questions that apply just to that industry They're not big picture big picture questions are good for any industry like, you know Here's an example of a big picture question, you know in terms of your current supplier What are the feet? What are you like? What are the best features right now with the current supplier? Most people will have like if you're selling cars or a board of car in the past I'll be driving one right now if you're essentially selling Hosting services. They'll say what do you like about your current hosting service right now? You know, what are the best features? You know, you want to get that out a big picture question that any Body will be able to answer. It's like not specific to an industry, but then you have Questions that only apply to your industry So the idea is because with some what so with those specific questions What you do is you before you ever pick up the phone or meet someone you want to know So what are the questions that I have to ask that are specific to my industry, right? And then based on these questions by the way These specific questions will be some will be non-invasive and so be invasive You get it See, it's like there's certain things that only someone in your industry will have to know and Some of those things will be Too personal to ask someone straight away because they'll be offended In the stock market one specific question is how much money do you have in the stock market right now? If I'm a dentist, I don't have to fucking ask that. That's a specific question to the stock market But if I'm a dentist or a stock broker, I'd like to what do you like with your current dentist? What do you like with your current? What do you have your current broker? This that's a big picture question applies to everyone So when you have these specific questions, you want to basically list them first and then divide them up into Non-invasive questions and invasive questions and you'll start off with your non-invasive questions first So what you're basically doing is you're using these non-invasive invasive questions to warm up the prospect to build massive rapport Take a sip here And then Once you've built more rapport you start digging in deeper with invasive questions Okay, now let's go to these big picture questions and these are sort of the to me are the keys to the kingdom for Great prospecting they really set you up to close in a massive way and There's really two of them that to me are the best ones of all there's a third that sort of go dovetails with it number one Okay, these are your most important big picture questions number one. What do you like? Whether it's a program a product or good it says whatever is what do you like about it? That's really important to know because the man of house much so might hate someone something a world a certain aspect They like and you want to know those things for two reasons one Because you want to make sure that when you explain what your offering is that you have those things Your product good or service is gonna have the features that they like about their old source They have a current source you you're your product good or service has those features On the flip side of the equation Okay, the next thing the next aspect is gonna be you want to make sure not only does it have it? Okay? But on the flip side what is wrong with their current source? What's wrong? What do they dislike basically? You ask the follow-up question So if they don't volunteer if they don't connect that back to themselves Or what it's costing the company then you ask the follow-up question Make sense and that's how you start ferreting out pain And if it's something that's really profound and important keep asking more follow-up questions You want to essentially amplify pain at this point? Uh-huh, and how do you see him? And how long is that been going on for? Mm-hmm? Yep. Oh my god. Yeah Yep, I totally get it. Yep. I hear that a lot. Mm-hmm. Yep. Whatever whatever the again just this is where flexibility Comes into play you have to just like if the straight line gives you this sort of perfect blueprint The how you move some from the open to the close, right? But in these little spots here I I can't unless I'm hired personally to come and say do this do that What I'm trying to say is you need to take a very close look at these key spots here and design your intelligence gathering to Get that exact outcome meaning to find out where their pain lies Amplify it if they're in denial and a lot of times they won't be though Just fucking start talking the other day we did a role play and it came out that the customer service was terrible to competitor and the Person who was doing the role but you're starting on my God is causing my job is bad my bosses And they in that case you wouldn't have to amplify playing they did it for you but very often they'll stop short of that and they'll just tell you what the problem is and Maybe not say how it really personally Impacts them or the company so in that case you would ask the follow-up question And then once they start to explain that you can then dig in further with another question to really make sure they understand What it's really cost them okay, so that's your second question then your third so what is what are you like to what are you dislike and number three? Okay, you know if you could design The product Yourself you can design so what would it into none? Give me the perfect solution for you now again This is not always going to apply to every industry But in many industries if they're using a product or program already and It makes sense to ask this question ask it Because what they'll do is they well I'd like to have this this and that they'll actually design a solution for you and Once they do that meant when you then make your presentation you're golden Okay, but again if it doesn't make sense don't don't feel compelled to ask this third question No, just design the program and you're selling so so design the cloth yourself. What the fuck? Power windows doesn't always make sense But in an insurance policy with a certain many different features they can add or not They'll tell you all this would be the best to be flexible of payment this room So you have to be smart about this but most of the time These first two questions are going to come into play. What do you like about it? What do you dislike about it? But remember it's not going to be the first question you ask them There's going to be specific Questions that are industry related questions that some intelligence you have to gather to really get contacts to understand What's going on in the prospects life right now? Once you have that completed then you go into sort of yours, you know And in terms of like what do you like about your current supply then without the context is you almost like You know, you could put your foot in your mouth very easily if you don't know what's going on So just be careful, but these are crucial big picture questions. Okay, that's that's your anchor points while you're prospecting right and then What happens that's what you next anchor your next anchor F. That's gonna be your transition so you're gonna have this point in your sale where no matter What happened? No matter how off-balance you might have felt no matter how awkward you might have been here And you can sometimes, you know, you just you're not on your you're not having your best day You feel awkward you feel confused just remember that there's going to be this Transitional phrase that you make that connects your intelligence gathering to your presentation Right. So your intelligence gathering is designed to do what? To identify where their pain lies What their problems are that's the purpose of intelligence and do they qualify for my product? Can I help them? There's my product suit their needs and be can they afford it? Right now just one point here when you transition just remember this the transition is meant to connect The intelligence gathering to the main presentation, right? But one important point here is that you're not looking to take every single person you speak to and Continue moving them down the straight line to the close. It's not what you want to do And this is a mistake that novices make all the time. They try to close everybody just as important as connecting your Intelligence gathering to your presentation is the fact that you have to weed out the people who aren't right for your product In other words remember from from general prospect is general straight line prospecting, you know The matter how hard you try in terms of your targeting your advertising and your marketing, right? You know, let's say you bring in 100 people if 100 people come into your pipeline hundred people right Not all hundred are gonna be right to your product And not all hundred are gonna have the money to buy your product The mistake that people make is that when they just get started says they want to just fucking pitch everybody So let's say you are actual prelly turn the page or say watch so I'll draw the straight line Okay, this is your open. This is your clothes, right? And you have your dotted lines above is your boundaries, right? So in those first four seconds, you are gonna what take immediate control then over those next 10 You're establishing that you the conscious mind now Wow, yeah, he really does sound a real deal and that's gonna lead you into this intelligence gathering phase So you get intelligence gather and do it in a way that builds master report You get that so your gathering intelligence and building massive rapport now At a certain point when you know what you need to know You asked all the questions you need to write you have your straight line transition and that phrase in the You know the symbols, you know, Jill John based on what you said to me this program is this is a perfect big deal What here's what I recommend. Let me tell you why Sorry that's that's what your transition is but also Watch what happens because if you do that and you make a presentation that presentation might be for Well five minutes long or longer not much longer Hopefully right you're gonna front load but the point you're gonna have four five six seven minutes of a presentation and Then all the back and forth that comes at that with objective error What if you decide that you know, I don't care what they know I'm gonna ask these questions no matter what I'm gonna try to pitch everyone happens is in the real world of these hundred people about half Are gonna be right for your product and half are totally utterly worthless. They don't qualify financially They don't really need you probably they were dragged into your pipeline by accidents or there those dreaded tire kickers Those look you lose. We're just there to sort of window shop. They have no intention of buying So what happens is if you don't make a conscious effort here to weed these people out You're gonna end up making an extra 30 or 40 presentations To people that you should never in the reason I didn't make the full 50s was some were always gonna slip through So you cannot read out every tire kicker that's impossible You can read out a lot when you get good at this and for that I'll say go back to know I don't want to get to the nitty-gritty how you do that But I've explained that pull do that later on again But you can go back in time for another training just Google or just go into a search function off I've explained that before the point is is that when you are Profession at this at we now tire kickers you can't read them all out some still gonna slip through that's fine That's why you will never close everyone you speak to but the point is you can eliminate at least 75% of these Because if you don't do that what happens you're making these six to ten minute long presentations to people of no fucking chance of buying and Then number one you waste time and more important know what happens a fuck. I don't understand what's wrong with me I gave this great presentation They seem like they were interested but they just didn't buy no fucking side couldn't close me a lot buyers You get it. So my point is this is anchor point here this transitional phrase John Jill let me just say that they are based on what you just based on what you said to me This is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why or Jill John based on what you said to me It's obviously it's not right for you or you really can't afford it's not in the right person I suggest you go here instead. It could just as well be that now hopefully it's not but what you don't want to do is Become so rigid here that this is like my train. I gotta just execute my transition your transition is Designed to weed people out as well. You know Jill based on what you said honestly This is really not a good fit here. You don't really need it. Also. It's kind of above your your you know What you could afford now I suggest that you go here or so we'll call back later you get it now You don't want to be over cautious Right, but you certainly don't want to talk to me simply that you said I can't afford your private I thought this way I had a guy who's a hard for very short time when I was first getting started here It's a calling out bound right as much as it was dabbling with it and I overheard a second call That's a taper call and the guy started off the call because he knew the straight line saying I just want to let you know I have no money. I can't afford this product Right and then my sales are starting to try to spend 15 minutes selling the fuck I fired the guy that what the fuck's for you The guy just told you he said he said to I don't want to waste your time and you pitched him anyway Why would you do that? You're wasting your time the company's time and you're pissing the person off So the straight line transition here is designed to not just connect the Intelligence guy into Presentation giving it's all sorts of get rid of people who aren't right for the product But those people who are right are gonna make their transition and you're gonna give them a straight line presentation so now let's talk about the Ability of what that really means. Okay, what does it mean to be flexible with a straight line presentation? Well number one Okay, you know The general rule here and there's all general rules was everything's a big difference, but the general rules of making presentations I've given you another train about you know things like your don't front load We're not alchemists. We don't like you. You can't turn non buyers into buyers Link your goods or services to trustworthy figures paint the picture use metaphors Civilians don't just be dry Right now talk about energy in benefits out of the end Urgency checking you into all these guys. There's just good on the on the back end of the system Just say Presentations and you'll see four or five intensives Look at the one where I don't want to duplicate what you already did where I lay out the actual rules of making presentations That's already here. I want to talk about something else right now And that is how do you take what you're doing and put it into this framework? That's the better question here, right? Because we already know the basics here the higher level is okay If you know if you know you're supposed to check in with the prospect me you don't want to just talk for five minutes straight You guys say make sense to you. You guys is here. Okay, great. You move forward You know you have to basically tell stories versus just dry facts reciting, right? You know you have to whenever possible link your service to a trustworthy figure. Okay, so all these things are basics, right? But the question is how do you apply this to your? Actual business to your actual script. Let me tell you how to do this the best way I've seen for people if you're working as a group Okay, you can do this as a team if not you can do individually But I want you to do I said this last week Do you know what I want to dig into this more deeply is I want you to start off by Essentially writing out your whole presentation in one shot from open To close meaning I want you to essentially just start off with a kitchen sink approach Where you see you know I want to make my warm-winded Front-loaded presentation if all the features all that is all the awesome stories I could tell all the Customers I could throw in those trustworthy figures I could link all that shit in one line and that presentation might be 25 minutes long It might be 15 minutes on whatever is but you'll want you to write out the whole thing in order first Okay, and don't worry about front-loading. That's step one then step two is gonna be to to start dividing it up Divided into three presentations in other words say to yourself, okay imagine if you're building a home right your house What do you do first? Well, you don't put up the fucking drywall before you pour the foundation and put up the frame right you put the foundation first Then the frame Right, then you put the plumbing and electric in then you put the drywall then you paint it then you lay out the car There's a certain way you go about building a home right the same thing is true when you are making a case For how you present your product all right, so I want you to just take a very logical step back here so write the whole thing out in one shot first and then Consciously say okay What is the best way to essentially frame? What's the the big picture story of how I explain someone? Logically why my product makes sense and it's gotta be based on logic That's the key your first presentation is logical. It's big picture. You're not front-loading start there And then when as you're doing that you take you you know, you don't take your eyes and typically what I do I don't take my absolute best stories I usually my best stories for my second, but I have some really solid stories I tell I frame this program really really carefully It's a sort of overall frame that makes sense and then the reason I leave out this better stuff here It's because I know that no matter how hard I try to sound awesome to sound You know absolutely certain most people 98% of time they're gonna say sounds good join. Let me think about it Most people hit you with an objection the vast majority So why would you give all your best stuff up front? See what what happens with a lot of salespeople is they There's so I Wouldn't say terrorized or terrified, but they're so sick and tired of Hearing those dreaded words. Let me think about it. Let me call you back They're like, I just if I could just if I could just sound so good or if I could just make such an airtight case I can avoid my prospect saying that I Got bad news here. You can't they fucking say that matter what you do. That's how people are built Most people hit you with objections No, if they don't have no just conjure one up people have objections and there's smoke screens for uncertainty so What you're doing essentially you're in your own mind you're reframing What it really means to finish a presentation and ask for the order for the first time when you ask for the order for the first time You're not expecting a yes and as soon as you can sort of you know get that really Indulterated into your psychology your heart and soul. I've got you know, I'm matter what I do I'm probably not gonna get an okay. Yeah, let's do it when I asked for the order the first time I'm probably gonna get it here. Yeah, it sounds good. You'll let me think about it and by having yourself prepared that's not just as expected it gives you the emotional where it all to essentially say John I'm for me. I'm ready. I'm wrong. I think great. Finally. I got that first objection I can now roll up my sleeves and earn my paycheck That's the mindset of a straight line salesperson because he let me just tell you something flat out You get paid big money Not for closing the easy ones you don't get rich as a salesperson for closing people who are pre-sold You know a perfect straight line sales like that guys like oh, yeah, great. Yeah, it's yeah. Yeah. Yeah, awesome Oh my god sounds good. You make a presentation. Yes. Yes. Yes. They yes the whole way through That's a perfect straight line sale. They don't object and when you ask for the order for the first time they say, oh my god Yeah, let's do it. That's a perfect straight line sale No one is gonna pay you a lot of money for those people those people are pre-sold before they ever entered your pipeline If you want to make a lot of money as a salesperson The way you do that is by being able to take the ones who don't lay down Who don't say oh my god. Yeah, let's do it. Anyone could close those The commission on those people is very small, but what business owners? Entrepreneurs will pay a ton of money for is someone who could take up. Let me think about it Let me call you back and turn that person into a yes. That's the magic You get it. That's what you get paid for So if that's your mindset in your mind, you're just you're doing everything right from the first word out of your mouth To that moment you asked for the order for the first meal everything was not exactly according to play right highest level But it's not because you expected the outcome of that to be and because I did everything right He bought from me or she bought that's gonna be very tough way to go through life as a salesperson rather It's I did everything right. I'm in tight rapport. They totally perceive me as an expert I sounded awesome. I framed an amazing airtight logical case So now they say let me think of that so now I can go about doing my job and closing the deal I'm waiting for that first objection and that's what you need to be to you need expecting and embracing it and Eating it for fucking breakfast. It's fucking easy to overcome objections That's all they are is smoke screens for uncertainty And remember this is ethical sales and persuasion. You're helping someone get something they need So now benefit them so they'll help them solve a problem relieve a pain point So you're doing the right thing but people have been conditioned with pre-program not to say yes right away Now what I see again, I'm gonna go back to the original point here first I ask you go to Q&A is that What happens is if you are too rigid in your application of the straight line system, you don't Along the way you fall out of rapport you lose that position as expert You don't accomplish those little building blocks. I said you're putting these building blocks in place So the whole thing's designed So you get to the point when you ask for the orders so they could say let me think about it Is that a good answer? You're expecting it. You want it you embrace it two more minutes Well, I'll be done right and we can also do in Q&A the point is everything you do Everything you say is not designed so you get someone to say yeah, let's do it when you ask for the order It's designed so they say damn that guy's really sharp that loud. I'm a good presentation I'm not buying it right now, but damn this guy is good and in your heart. You're waiting for this first objection That's the key and the flexibility I'm talking about is applying all those rules and principles and strategies of the straight line So you are perceived as an expert the whole way through those first four seconds, right? It's an unconscious pattern You said something you sounded a certain way you'll look to certain things like like Guy seems like he's really sharp. I'm gonna listen so they listen But then after those first four seconds to hang it on your every word their eyes are now looking at your body language They're saying is he the real deal or is he bullshit? Is she bullshit? You get it. That's what's going on. So you need to have that second pattern Figure it out locked down tight How you describe your company everything you say? That's how you would That's how you would Lower the accession in terms of raising the pain threshold, you know say, you know doc I you know I understand that you know you said before that you don't you're finding it more and more difficult to get good quality Customers because getting so competitive, you know, honestly, I'm doing this for a long time right now Okay, things are only thing to get a much more competitive. Where do you see this happening in two years? I want everybody else was crowned in space. Tell the guy Where do you see this going if you don't make a decision right now? You think your business is gonna get better on its own? More the same you know my the definition of insanity doc is You know doing the same thing over and over again expecting a different result and right now the result you're going to get It's causing you massive pain. I'm here to help you resolve that pain And tell the guy, you know, you're not it's not gonna fix itself this problem Where do you see yourself in two years from now or a year from now when the space gets more and more crowded people are Literally, you know taking away your existing customers. They know, I mean, it's not a pretty picture But by positioning your business the right way online first page of Google getting traffic going steady way You turn yourself into an industry leader in your market and over the short term animal. That's what you do Okay. All right, now Marius Muse says is there a way to find out the auto you're just out there. Um, Chevy What's up guy Chevy asks? What's up guys? I really wouldn't okay What it's got to be about is that people you have a good product and not only You want to recruit people to sign up be on your front line? But they got to be selling the product at the end of the day or else just a pyramid scheme That's the overwhelming thing. Okay. All right, David asked David penne asked my question is how would you how would you frame that for? Private equity that he says that one to convince business owners to sell the company to you So well, you know remember The key is you always want to be walking in the customer shoes your company, right? You know, what are the biggest fears that these companies have of taking in private equity? Well, they till they're gonna lose control Right, you know, you know, they're gonna be told what to do. They you know, they lose their independence and so forth So the key here with private equities, you know, you're both your second 10 annual third 10, you know, what what? The first 10 is kind of pretty basic, right? You know, I'm gonna give you a few million dollars in jet cap. You know, that's an easy one to see why it's a great one day We can go public the upside of the first 10 is very easy It's the second and third 10 with private equity it really gonna ship you up So you got to focus on the fact that number one, you'll be there with them to hold their hand every step of the way and the company We want to be a strategic partner with you over the long term help you grow your business We're not there to help you. We're not there to cubby-hole you and kind of you know Take away that entrepreneurial spirit and that made you great in the first is we want to actually facilitate open up doors for you Focus on that the ancillary benefits of having a partner that can open up doors for you provided with capital That's what you want to focus on. I really got a couple more questions But I think we should get to the other ones as well from last week. So that's one more Angela creative entrepreneurs as any tips of differentiating yourself from competitors during the presentation of the product Should I compare myself to competitors? No, there's nothing I mean to a point in other words, again, that's an industry specific thing If you have major competitors, right? Obviously you want to focus on the things that That make your product or service much better and the competitors I wouldn't go about bashing competitors I don't believe in doing that. So you want to kind of frame things more from the positive side, right? But if there are obvious competitors that you can mention them and explain why your product is better But I wouldn't go about bashing competitors. I don't think it's gonna be a good thing to do I find I find like usually they bring it up the customers will bring up which it thoughts Anyway, so this is from last week now Deb Jeter child Harry says I guess I'm so sorry for the last thing I feel like I usually can't get away with more than one or two loops when lead generating for appointments in person They start backing away or being more dismissive. Is there anything I can do or is that expected? Well, would you make would you make someone be able to get away with more loops? Well, the magic pattern there typically is when someone is starting to get sort of upset about you looping is that they feel pressured Right, so the typical pattern you want to throw is again Jill John, Mr. Jones, please don't misconstrue my enthusiasm for pressure Okay, it's just I know this is a perfect view and I can help you out So that and they say but again all I want to do right now is send you some information So you use that to sort of you know, I don't misconstrue my enthusiasm for pressure That's the pattern you want to use there, but again if someone's feeling pressure, you know You just did I told about this in the way of the wolf book, right? There's two options. You can use that to fuse Okay, and set up the next appointment. All right, or you can loop again and try one more time But also I would look back at the actual what's caused building You should be able to get more than two loops probably a third loop you focus on your tonality Keep anything short. Maybe you're front-loading a bit. So I look at both of those Awesome. All right next one comes in from Andrew Pierce Would you use objection handling in your case before the prospect even has the chance to use them during the close?
